data.title: Q1 Profit At ELEMENTS (TSE:5246) Tests Bearish Narratives Around Persistent TTM Losses
data.description:
ELEMENTS (TSE:5246) opened Q1 2026 with revenue of ¥1,291 million and basic EPS of ¥3.68, alongside net income of ¥100 million, providing a clearer starting point for assessing how the business is converting top line into per share results. The company’s quarterly revenue moved from ¥715 million in Q1 2025 to ¥1,291 million in Q1 2026, while EPS shifted from a loss of ¥2.08 to a profit of ¥3.68 over the same period, which puts the latest margin profile in focus for investors evaluating this change in earnings.

See our full analysis for ELEMENTS.

With the headline numbers reported, the next step is to compare these results with the most widely held narratives about ELEMENTS to see which views are supported and which ones the latest margins call into question.

TTM losses of ¥549 million put Q1 profit in context

  • Across the trailing 12 months, ELEMENTS recorded total revenue of ¥4,471 million and a net loss of ¥549 million, which sits alongside the Q1 2026 single quarter net income of ¥100 million.
  • What stands out for a bearish view is that the trailing 12 month basic EPS is a loss of ¥21.48 even after the latest quarter’s basic EPS of ¥3.68,
    • bears highlight that losses have grown at about 4.4% per year over the past five years, and the TTM loss figure aligns with that longer trend,
    • while the move from a loss of ¥51 million in Q1 2025 to a profit of ¥100 million in Q1 2026 sits against a backdrop where the TTM net loss remains sizable.

P/S of 4.4x sits between industry and peers

  • The current P/S multiple of 4.4x is higher than the Japan Software industry average of 1.6x, yet lower than the peer group average of 5.5x.
  • Critics argue the higher P/S versus the broader industry is hard to reconcile with ongoing losses,
    • because the company is still unprofitable over the last 12 months with a TTM net loss of ¥549 million and basic EPS of ¥21.48 in losses,
    • while supporters may point to the revenue base of ¥4,471 million over the same period as a reason the P/S sits closer to the 5.5x peer level than to the 1.6x industry average.

Volatile share price on a loss-making base

  • The share price is ¥732, and this has been described as highly volatile over the past three months compared with the Japan market while the company remained loss making over the trailing year.
  • For a bearish narrative, the combination of high volatility and a TTM loss of ¥549 million reinforces concerns around risk,
    • as losses have been increasing at about 4.4% annually over five years, which matches the picture given by the negative TTM basic EPS of ¥21.48,
    • and without forward guidance in the provided data, attention is likely to stay on how future quarters compare with this recent pattern of volatility paired with unprofitability.

data.title: Fastenal (FAST) Earnings Growth And 15.3% Margin Test Rich Valuation Narrative
data.description:
Fastenal (FAST) just posted its Q1 2026 scorecard, with recent quarterly numbers showing Q4 2025 revenue of about US$2.0b and basic EPS of US$0.26, alongside trailing twelve month basic EPS of US$1.10 on revenue of roughly US$8.2b. The company has seen revenue move from US$1.82b in Q4 2024 to about US$2.03b in Q4 2025, while quarterly basic EPS shifted from US$0.23 to US$0.26 over the same period, setting up this latest report against a backdrop of steady top line and per share earnings. With net profit margins holding in the mid teens, investors are likely to focus on how Fastenal is converting sales into profits as they digest these results.

See our full analysis for Fastenal.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings run rate lines up with the widely followed bull and bear narratives around Fastenal and where those stories might need updating.

See what the community is saying about Fastenal

Advertisement

9.4% earnings growth and 15.3% margin go under the microscope

  • Over the last 12 months, Fastenal generated US$8.2b of revenue and US$1.26b of net income, which works out to earnings growth of 9.4% and a net profit margin of 15.3% compared with 15.2% a year earlier.
  • Supporters of the bullish narrative point to this combination of solid profit growth and stable margins as evidence that investments in on site services and digital tools are paying off, yet the modest 0.1 percentage point margin change and forecast earnings growth of about 8% a year show only a gradual improvement, not the step change in profitability bulls often talk about.
    • Bulls highlight growing Fastenal Managed Inventory and vending, which they expect to push margins higher, but the trailing 15.3% margin is only slightly above the prior 15.2%, so the impact is visible but limited in the reported numbers.
    • With revenue projected to grow 7.8% a year compared with a 10.6% rate cited for the broader US market, the current earnings trend supports the bullish story on resilience, yet the growth pace is not outpacing the wider market the way more aggressive expectations imply.
Fastenal’s recent profit trend looks solid, but bulls who see a bigger breakaway story may want to check how that view lines up with the detailed growth and margin assumptions in the dedicated bull case narrative 🐂 Fastenal Bull Case.

Premium 41.8x P/E and US$21.38 DCF fair value gap

  • The stock trades on a trailing P/E of 41.8x compared with 24.1x for peers and 22.9x for the broader US Trade Distributors group, while the supplied DCF fair value of US$21.38 sits well below the current share price of US$45.80.
  • Critics in the bearish camp focus on this valuation gap and argue that if earnings growth slows toward their 7.4% annual assumption and margins ease from 15.3% to 15.1%, the current 41.8x multiple could be hard to justify, especially when analyst price targets cluster around US$46.33.
    • Bears point to tariff and sourcing risks that could pressure margins, and even a 0.2 percentage point move from 15.3% to 15.1% would matter when the share price already stands at more than double the DCF fair value of US$21.38.
    • The fact that the analyst target band compresses around US$46.33 while the trailing P/E remains far above the 22.9x industry average backs the cautious view that expectations built into today’s price leave less room for disappointment.
If you are weighing this premium P/E against the cautious narratives on tariffs and growth, it is worth seeing how skeptics frame the downside case in the dedicated bear case breakdown 🐻 Fastenal Bear Case.

Dividend yield 2.1% with tight free cash flow cover

  • Fastenal’s dividend yield sits at 2.1%, and the analysis notes that free cash flow does not comfortably cover that payout, which means investors are relying on continued US$1.26b earnings and cash conversion to keep funding those distributions.
  • Analysts’ consensus narrative sees the company leaning on expanding Fastenal Managed Inventory, higher digital sales, and supply chain diversification to support both earnings and the dividend over time, yet the combination of slower forecast revenue growth of 7.8% a year and rich valuation metrics creates a tension between income appeal and balance sheet flexibility.
    • The 2.1% yield may look attractive compared with cash, but limited free cash flow cover means any pressure on the 15.3% margin or on revenue growth could quickly reduce the room to fund both dividends and ongoing investments.
    • Because the share price at US$45.80 is close to the consolidated analyst target of around US$46.33, income focused investors are not only exposed to payout risk from cash flow constraints but also have less of a valuation cushion if growth or margins come in below expectations.

Next Steps

The mix of solid earnings, premium valuation and a tightly covered dividend will mean different things to different investors. Take a moment to stress test the thesis against both upside potential and downside risks by reviewing the 2 key rewards and 1 important warning sign.

data.description:

The company has seen revenue move from ¥156.0b to ¥163.0b on a trailing 12 month basis, while trailing EPS shifted from ¥295.58 to ¥268.55, setting up a picture where top line progress sits alongside softer earnings and margins that investors may want to weigh carefully.

See our full analysis for Hisamitsu Pharmaceutical.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to consider how this mix of revenue growth and margin pressure aligns with the prevailing stories investors follow about Hisamitsu Pharmaceutical and to assess where those stories might be tested by the latest figures.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

Margins Ease Back To 11.8%

  • Trailing net profit margin sits at 11.8%, compared with 13.9% a year earlier, alongside trailing 12 month net income of ¥19,160 million on ¥163,024 million of revenue.
  • Critics highlight the bearish angle that softer profitability weakens the case for a premium valuation, and the margin slip from 13.9% to 11.8% does give that view some support.
    • The margin shift comes while trailing EPS moved from ¥295.58 to ¥268.55, so the business is currently converting revenue into earnings at a lower rate than a year ago.
    • At the same time, revenue on a trailing 12 month basis has risen from ¥156,006 million to ¥163,024 million, so the pressure looks more about costs and mix than about demand disappearing.
On this set of numbers, skeptics may focus on the margin drift, while others will watch to see if profitability stabilizes before reconsidering their stance on the share price premium relative to peers. 🐻 Hisamitsu Pharmaceutical Bear Case

Five Year EPS Growth Versus Latest Year

  • Five year earnings growth averaged 16.4% per year, yet over the last 12 months earnings have moved backwards compared with the prior year, with trailing net profit margin easing from 13.9% to 11.8%.
  • What stands out for bullish arguments is the contrast between that 16.4% long run earnings track and the current year of weaker earnings, which bulls may treat as a pause rather than a lasting change.
    • The latest trailing 12 month EPS of ¥268.55 sits below the prior ¥295.58, even as the five year average growth rate remains solid, so the recent softness sits against a stronger multi year backdrop.

data.title: Concerns Surrounding S.C. Iproeb's (BVB:IPRU) Performance
data.description:
The market for S.C. Iproeb S.A.'s (BVB:IPRU) stock was strong after it released a healthy earnings report last week. Despite this, our analysis suggests that there are some factors weakening the foundations of those good profit numbers.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

Zooming In On S.C. Iproeb's Earnings

Many investors haven't heard of the accrual ratio from cashflow, but it is actually a useful measure of how well a company's profit is backed up by free cash flow (FCF) during a given period. To get the accrual ratio we first subtract FCF from profit for a period, and then divide that number by the average operating assets for the period. This ratio tells us how much of a company's profit is not backed by free cashflow.

As a result, a negative accrual ratio is a positive for the company, and a positive accrual ratio is a negative. While it's not a problem to have a positive accrual ratio, indicating a certain level of non-cash profits, a high accrual ratio is arguably a bad thing, because it indicates paper profits are not matched by cash flow. Notably, there is some academic evidence that suggests that a high accrual ratio is a bad sign for near-term profits, generally speaking.

For the year to December 2025, S.C. Iproeb had an accrual ratio of 0.29. Unfortunately, that means its free cash flow was a lot less than its statutory profit, which makes us doubt the utility of profit as a guide. In the last twelve months it actually had negative free cash flow, with an outflow of RON35m despite its profit of RON19.5m, mentioned above. Coming off the back of negative free cash flow last year, we imagine some shareholders might wonder if its cash burn of RON35m, this year, indicates high risk.

Note: we always recommend investors check balance sheet strength. Click here to be taken to our balance sheet analysis of S.C. Iproeb.

Our Take On S.C. Iproeb's Profit Performance

S.C. Iproeb didn't convert much of its profit to free cash flow in the last year, which some investors may consider rather suboptimal. Therefore, it seems possible to us that S.C. Iproeb's true underlying earnings power is actually less than its statutory profit. In further bad news, its earnings per share decreased in the last year. The goal of this article has been to assess how well we can rely on the statutory earnings to reflect the company's potential, but there is plenty more to consider. So while earnings quality is important, it's equally important to consider the risks facing S.C. Iproeb at this point in time. To that end, you should learn about the 2 warning signs we've spotted with S.C. Iproeb (including 1 which is significant).
